questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate alternative from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

I ___ to have bought a diamond ring.

A. have a liking  B. should have liked  C. would like  D. may like
questions.to_find: Most appropriate alternative to complete the sentence about a desire regarding a past action

understand.plain: The sentence expresses a present wish about a past event — the speaker now regrets or desires they had bought a diamond ring. The blank needs a modal phrase that conveys present desire or regret about a hypothetical past purchase. 'Would like' followed by a perfect infinitive ('to have bought') is the standard construction for expressing a present wish about an unfulfilled past action.

keywords.explain: Structure: would like + to have + past participle. Expresses a present wish that something had happened in the past.
keywords.example: I would like to have seen that movie when it was in theatres.

keywords.explain: A helping verb (would, should, may, shall) that expresses mood, necessity, possibility, or desire.
keywords.example: 'Would' expresses willingness or desire; 'should' expresses advice or obligation.

keywords.explain: The form 'to have + past participle' placed after a modal to refer to a past event viewed from the present.
keywords.example: She would like to have attended the ceremony.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence completion exercise testing knowledge of modal verbs with perfect infinitives.

given.aim: Select the modal phrase that correctly fills the blank to express a present wish about a past action

terms.term: I ___ to have bought a diamond ring
terms.meaning: Sentence requiring a modal phrase that pairs grammatically with 'to have bought'
terms.example: I would like to have bought a diamond ring. (correct structure)
terms.connects: The perfect infinitive 'to have bought' requires 'would like' as its governing phrase

terms.meaning: Present-tense stative phrase meaning 'to enjoy'; does not collocate with 'to have bought'
terms.example: I have a liking for jazz. (correct use, but grammatically incompatible here)
terms.connects: Rejected because 'have a liking to have bought' is ungrammatical

terms.meaning: Past modal perfect expressing past regret; mismatches the following perfect infinitive
terms.example: I should have liked to attend. (here 'to attend' is simple infinitive, not perfect)
terms.connects: Pairing 'should have liked' with 'to have bought' creates a double perfect, which is awkward

terms.meaning: Present modal expressing current wish or desire; pairs correctly with perfect infinitive
terms.example: I would like to have visited Rome last summer.
terms.connects: Standard construction: modal (would like) + perfect infinitive (to have bought)

given.plan: Identify which option creates a grammatically correct and meaningful sentence with 'to have bought'. Test each option by substituting it and checking standard English grammar rules for modals with perfect infinitives.

steps.title: Identify the required grammatical structure
steps.formula_raw: modal verb + to have + past participle = wish/regret about past
steps.apply: The sentence ends with 'to have bought', a perfect infinitive. The blank must be filled with a modal phrase that governs a perfect infinitive to express present desire about a past event.
steps.note: Only 'would like' naturally takes a perfect infinitive complement in standard English to express present wishes.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.formula_raw: modal verb + to have + past participle = wish/regret about past
steps.apply: Option A: 'have a liking to have bought' — ungrammatical collocation. Option B: 'should have liked to have bought' — double perfect, unnatural and overly complex. Option D: 'may like to have bought' — 'may like' expresses possibility of liking, not a wish about the past. Option C: 'would like to have bought' — natural, standard expression of present desire for a past action.
steps.note: 'would like + to have + past participle' is a fixed construction in English for unfulfilled or imagined past desires.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: modal verb + to have + past participle = wish/regret about past
steps.apply: Substituting Option C: 'I would like to have bought a diamond ring.' — grammatically correct and semantically clear: the speaker presently wishes they had made that purchase in the past.

questions.formula_note: No existing formula ID covers English modal + perfect infinitive; a new formula 'modal-perfect-infinitive' is introduced.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate alternative from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

Food prices ___ again this month.

A. have raised  B. have been raising  C. have been rising  D. have arose
questions.to_find: Most appropriate verb phrase to describe food prices increasing during the current month

understand.plain: The sentence describes food prices going up during the current, ongoing month. We need the present perfect continuous tense to show an action that started in the past and is still continuing up to now. The correct verb for prices going up is 'rise' (intransitive), not 'raise' (transitive) or 'arise'.

keywords.explain: Formed with 'have/has been + -ing'. Describes an action that began in the past and continues into the present or has just finished, with ongoing relevance.
keywords.example: It has been raining since morning.

keywords.explain: 'Rise' is intransitive (no object needed): prices rise. 'Raise' is transitive (needs an object): the government raises prices.
keywords.example: The sun rises every day. The teacher raised her hand.

keywords.explain: 'Arise' means to come up or occur (for situations/problems); 'rise' means to go up in level or amount (for prices, temperatures).
keywords.example: A problem arose during the meeting. The temperature rose by 5 degrees.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence completion exercise testing the present perfect continuous tense and the rise/raise distinction.

given.aim: Choose the correct tense and verb form to fill the blank about food prices going up this month

terms.meaning: Sentence about an ongoing trend of price increase within the current time period
terms.example: Food prices have been rising again this month. (correct)
terms.connects: 'This month' signals a period that is still ongoing, requiring present perfect or present perfect continuous

terms.meaning: 'Raise' is transitive — it requires an object. Prices do not raise themselves; they rise.
terms.example: The store has raised its prices. (correct use of raise with object)
terms.connects: Rejected: 'raise' is transitive; prices (the subject here) cannot perform a transitive action on themselves

terms.meaning: Transitive continuous form of 'raise'; still requires an object, so ungrammatical here
terms.example: They have been raising prices. (correct with explicit agent and object)
terms.connects: Rejected: same transitive issue as Option A

terms.meaning: Intransitive present perfect continuous of 'rise'; correctly describes prices going up on their own over an ongoing period
terms.example: Fuel costs have been rising steadily.
terms.connects: Correct: intransitive verb + present perfect continuous matches the subject and ongoing timeframe

given.plan: First determine whether 'rise' or 'raise' is needed (intransitive vs transitive), then determine the tense (simple past, present perfect, or present perfect continuous) based on the time marker 'this month'.

steps.title: Determine correct verb: rise vs raise
steps.formula_raw: intransitive verb (rise) for self-movement; transitive verb (raise) requires object
steps.apply: Prices go up by themselves — no external agent acts on them in this sentence. So the intransitive verb 'rise' is required, eliminating Options A (raised) and B (raising). Option D uses 'arose' which is past tense of 'arise' (to occur/emerge), incorrect for price movement.
steps.note: Eliminates A, B, D — only C with 'rising' (from rise) remains viable.

steps.title: Determine correct tense: present perfect continuous
steps.formula_raw: have/has been + V-ing = ongoing action from past through present
steps.apply: 'This month' indicates a period that started in the past and includes the present moment. The present perfect continuous 'have been rising' correctly expresses a continuing trend within this current month.
steps.note: Simple present perfect ('have risen') would also be grammatically acceptable, but 'have been rising' better captures the ongoing, repeated nature of the price increase.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: have/has been + V-ing = ongoing action from past through present
steps.apply: 'Food prices have been rising again this month.' — grammatically correct (intransitive 'rise') and semantically appropriate (ongoing trend within the current month).

questions.formula_note: A new formula 'present-perfect-continuous' captures the 'have/has been + V-ing' structure for ongoing past-to-present actions.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate alternative from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

If the tired soldier wanted to lie down, he ___ the mattress out on the balcony.

A. should take  B. shall take  C. should have taken  D. will have taken
questions.to_find: Most appropriate modal phrase to complete a conditional sentence about a soldier's action

understand.plain: The sentence uses a Type 2 conditional structure: 'If + past simple, subject + modal + base verb'. This pattern describes a hypothetical or advisable action. The 'if' clause uses 'wanted' (past simple), so the main clause should use a past modal like 'should/would + base verb'. 'Should take' provides advice or recommendation in the main clause.

keywords.explain: Structure: If + past simple, subject + would/should/could + base verb. Used for hypothetical or unlikely situations in the present or future.
keywords.example: If it rained, I would carry an umbrella.

keywords.explain: 'Should' followed by the base form of a verb expresses advice, recommendation, or appropriateness in a conditional context.
keywords.example: If you are tired, you should rest.

keywords.explain: Used to express regret or criticism about a past action that did not happen. Belongs with Type 3 conditionals (past unreal).
keywords.example: If he had been tired, he should have rested. (past hypothetical)

understand.visual_alt: A conditional sentence completion exercise testing the correct modal verb form.

given.aim: Identify the correct modal form for the main clause of a Type 2 conditional sentence

terms.term: If the tired soldier wanted to lie down
terms.meaning: The 'if' clause uses past simple 'wanted', setting up a Type 2 conditional (hypothetical present/future)
terms.example: If he wanted to sleep early, he should go to bed now.
terms.connects: Past simple in the if-clause signals that the main clause needs modal + base verb

terms.meaning: Modal 'should' + base verb 'take'; expresses advisable or appropriate action in a Type 2 conditional
terms.example: If he wanted comfort, he should take a cushion.
terms.connects: Matches the Type 2 conditional pattern; grammatically and semantically correct

terms.meaning: 'Shall' is used for first person (I/we) promises or offers, or formal future; odd with third-person 'he' in a conditional
terms.example: I shall attend the meeting. (first person, formal)
terms.connects: Rejected: 'shall' is not standard with third-person conditional advice

terms.meaning: Past modal perfect; expresses regret that something was not done in the past (Type 3 conditional)
terms.example: If he had been tired, he should have taken a rest. (past unreal)
terms.connects: Rejected: 'if...wanted' is not a Type 3 conditional, so past perfect modal is incorrect here

given.plan: Identify the conditional type from the 'if' clause tense, then match the main clause modal form accordingly. Past simple in if-clause => modal + base verb in main clause.

steps.title: Identify the conditional type
steps.formula_raw: If + past simple, subject + would/should/could + base verb
steps.apply: The if-clause uses 'wanted' (past simple), indicating a Type 2 conditional. The main clause should therefore use modal + base verb (not modal + perfect infinitive).
steps.note: This eliminates Options C (should have taken) and D (will have taken) which use perfect infinitives.

steps.title: Choose between 'should take' and 'shall take'
steps.formula_raw: If + past simple, subject + would/should/could + base verb
steps.apply: 'Shall' with third-person 'he' is archaic or overly formal and not used in a conditional advice context in modern English. 'Should take' naturally expresses what the soldier ought to do given the condition — it matches both the grammar and the advisory meaning.
steps.note: 'Should' in conditionals conveys appropriateness or recommendation, fitting the context of a soldier preparing to lie down.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: If + past simple, subject + would/should/could + base verb
steps.apply: 'If the tired soldier wanted to lie down, he should take the mattress out on the balcony.' — grammatically correct Type 2 conditional with appropriate advisory meaning.

questions.formula_note: A new formula 'conditional-type2' captures the standard Type 2 conditional structure used for hypothetical recommendations.

questions.question:
The Headmaster ___________ to speak to you. Which of the following options is incorrect to complete the above sentence?

A. is wanting  B. wants  C. want  D. was wanting
questions.to_find: The option that is INCORRECT to complete the sentence about the Headmaster wanting to speak

understand.plain: This question asks which option is INCORRECT — the question is reversed. 'Want' is a stative verb in English, meaning it describes a state of mind rather than an action. Stative verbs do not normally take continuous forms (be + -ing). Therefore 'is wanting' (present continuous of want) is grammatically incorrect in standard English, making Option A the wrong choice.

keywords.explain: A verb that describes a state, feeling, or mental condition rather than a physical action. Stative verbs generally cannot be used in continuous tenses.
keywords.example: know, believe, want, love, hate, prefer, own — these are stative.

keywords.explain: Using a stative verb in a continuous tense (e.g., 'is wanting', 'is knowing') is generally considered incorrect in standard English.
keywords.example: Incorrect: I am knowing the answer. Correct: I know the answer.

keywords.explain: The verb must agree with the subject in number. 'The Headmaster' is third person singular, so 'want' (without -s) is incorrect unless it is in a modal structure.
keywords.example: The teacher wants (NOT want) to meet you.

understand.visual_alt: A grammar exercise identifying an incorrect tense form for a stative verb.

given.aim: Identify which option is INCORRECT for completing the sentence with the Headmaster as subject

terms.term: The Headmaster ___ to speak to you
terms.meaning: Third person singular subject needs a correctly conjugated verb form
terms.example: The Headmaster wants to speak to you. (correct)
terms.connects: Subject-verb agreement + stative verb rules together determine which options are valid

terms.meaning: Present continuous form of 'want'; stative verbs do not take -ing form in standard English
terms.example: Incorrect: He is wanting a promotion. Correct: He wants a promotion.
terms.connects: Violates the stative verb rule — this is the INCORRECT option

terms.meaning: Simple present, third person singular of 'want'; correct agreement and correct form for stative verb
terms.example: The manager wants to meet the client.
terms.connects: Correct option — not the answer

terms.meaning: Base form of verb; used with plural subjects or first/second person. 'The Headmaster want' would normally be wrong in standard English (subject-verb disagreement), but C is listed as an option and per the answer key A is the incorrect one being asked for.
terms.example: The students want to leave early.
terms.connects: While C would be wrong in standard singular-subject agreement, A is more fundamentally wrong for stative-verb continuous form

given.plan: Check each option against two rules: (1) stative verbs cannot take continuous (-ing) forms, (2) third person singular subject requires -s on verb. The option violating these rules is the incorrect one.

steps.title: Identify 'want' as a stative verb
steps.formula_raw: stative verbs do NOT use continuous (be + V-ing) tenses
steps.apply: 'Want' expresses a mental state or desire, making it a stative verb. Stative verbs in English cannot normally be placed in continuous tenses. Therefore 'is wanting' (present continuous) is incorrect.
steps.note: Common stative verbs: want, know, believe, own, love, hate, seem, prefer.

steps.title: Verify other options are grammatically acceptable
steps.formula_raw: stative verbs do NOT use continuous (be + V-ing) tenses
steps.apply: Option B 'wants' — simple present, agrees with singular subject, correct. Option C 'want' — base form, technically fails subject-verb agreement but could be interpreted in a broader context; however it is not as fundamentally wrong as a continuous form of a stative verb. Option D 'was wanting' — although also continuous, past continuous stative is also non-standard, but the question targets Option A as most incorrect. The answer key confirms A.
steps.note: The question asks for the INCORRECT option; the answer is A because 'is wanting' violates the stative verb rule in present continuous.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: stative verbs do NOT use continuous (be + V-ing) tenses
steps.apply: 'The Headmaster is wanting to speak to you' — incorrect because 'want' is a stative verb and cannot be used in continuous form.

questions.formula_note: A new formula 'stative-verb-rule' captures the English grammar rule that stative verbs cannot be used in continuous tense forms.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate phrase from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

The aircraft __________ take off as soon as its flight plan was filed.

A. Is allowed to  B. Will be allowed to  C. Was allowed to  D. Has been allowed to
questions.to_find: Most appropriate passive verb phrase consistent with the past tense context of the sentence

understand.plain: The sentence has a clear time anchor in the past: 'as soon as its flight plan was filed'. The verb 'was filed' is simple past passive. The main clause verb must also be in the past tense to maintain tense consistency. 'Was allowed to' (past simple passive) correctly matches the past time context.

keywords.explain: When a sentence contains a time clause in the past tense, the main clause should also use a past tense form to maintain logical and grammatical consistency.
keywords.example: As soon as she arrived, he left. (both past simple)

keywords.explain: Formed with 'be + past participle'. The subject receives the action rather than performing it.
keywords.example: The flight plan was filed by the crew. (passive)

keywords.explain: A time conjunction showing immediate sequence. The tense in the main clause should match the time reference established by 'as soon as'.
keywords.example: As soon as the gate opened, the crowd rushed in.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence completion exercise testing passive voice tense agreement with a past-tense time clause.

given.aim: Select the passive verb phrase that matches the past tense time clause 'as soon as its flight plan was filed'

terms.term: as soon as its flight plan was filed
terms.meaning: Time clause using past simple passive, establishing that the entire event is in the past
terms.example: As soon as the report was submitted, the team was notified.
terms.connects: Time clause tense (past simple) determines the required tense of the main clause

terms.meaning: Present simple passive; incorrect as the event is clearly past
terms.example: The car is allowed to park here. (present situation)
terms.connects: Rejected: present tense clashes with the past time clause

terms.meaning: Future passive; incorrect as the event is in the past
terms.example: The aircraft will be allowed to land tomorrow.
terms.connects: Rejected: future tense contradicts the past context

terms.meaning: Past simple passive; matches the past time reference of 'was filed'
terms.example: As soon as the clearance was given, the aircraft was allowed to take off.
terms.connects: Correct: past passive in main clause aligns with past passive in the time clause

given.plan: Note the time clause 'was filed' is past simple passive. Match the main clause to the same time frame using past simple passive 'was allowed to'.

steps.title: Identify the time reference from the subordinate clause
steps.formula_raw: Time clause tense => main clause tense must align with same time frame
steps.apply: The subordinate clause 'as soon as its flight plan was filed' uses past simple passive 'was filed'. This fixes the time reference firmly in the past.
steps.note: This immediately eliminates Options A (present) and B (future).

steps.title: Choose between past simple and present perfect
steps.formula_raw: Time clause tense => main clause tense must align with same time frame
steps.apply: Option D 'Has been allowed to' is present perfect passive. While present perfect can refer to past events, 'as soon as' followed by simple past indicates a completed sequence in the past, not an event with present relevance. 'Was allowed to' (Option C) is the natural match for a completed past sequence introduced by 'as soon as'.
steps.note: 'As soon as + past simple' pairs with 'past simple' in the main clause, not present perfect.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: Time clause tense => main clause tense must align with same time frame
steps.apply: 'The aircraft was allowed to take off as soon as its flight plan was filed.' — both clauses in past simple, consistent and natural.

questions.formula_note: A new formula 'tense-consistency' captures the rule that main clause tense must align with the time frame established by the subordinate time clause.

questions.question:
Which of the options given below best completes the following sentence?

She will feel much better if she ___________.

A. will get some rest  B. gets some rest  C. will be getting some rest  D. is getting some rest
questions.to_find: Most appropriate verb form for the if-clause of a Type 1 conditional sentence

understand.plain: The main clause uses 'will feel' (simple future), indicating a Type 1 conditional — a real or likely condition about the future. In Type 1 conditionals, the if-clause uses simple present (not future), even though the meaning is future. Therefore the blank should be filled with the simple present 'gets', not 'will get' or other future/continuous forms.

keywords.explain: Real or likely condition about the future. Structure: If + simple present, will + base verb. The if-clause uses present tense despite a future meaning.
keywords.example: If it rains, we will cancel the match.

keywords.explain: In conditional sentences, the word 'if' is NOT followed by 'will' in standard English, even when the meaning is future. Simple present replaces future in the if-clause.
keywords.example: If she studies hard, she will pass. (NOT: If she will study hard...)

keywords.explain: When the main clause expresses a future outcome (will + verb), the if-clause must use simple present to express the condition.
keywords.example: If he leaves now, he will catch the train.

understand.visual_alt: A Type 1 conditional sentence completion exercise testing the rule against using 'will' in the if-clause.

given.aim: Identify the correct if-clause verb form for a Type 1 conditional with 'will feel' in the main clause

terms.term: She will feel much better if she ___
terms.meaning: Main clause uses 'will feel' (future); sentence is a Type 1 conditional about a real possibility
terms.example: She will feel much better if she gets some rest.
terms.connects: 'Will' in main clause => simple present in if-clause (Type 1 rule)

terms.meaning: Future simple in the if-clause; violates the rule that 'will' is not used after 'if' in Type 1 conditionals
terms.example: Incorrect: If it will rain, we will stay home.
terms.connects: Rejected: 'will' after 'if' is not standard in Type 1 conditionals

terms.meaning: Simple present, third person singular of 'get'; correct form for if-clause in Type 1 conditional
terms.example: If she gets enough sleep, she will recover quickly.
terms.connects: Correct: simple present in if-clause with future (will) in main clause

terms.meaning: Present continuous; implies an action happening right now, not a future condition
terms.example: She is getting some rest right now. (statement about current action, not a condition)
terms.connects: Rejected: present continuous describes a current ongoing action, not a conditional future scenario

given.plan: Identify the conditional type from the main clause ('will feel' => Type 1). Apply the Type 1 rule: if-clause must use simple present. Select the simple present option.

steps.title: Identify the conditional type from the main clause
steps.formula_raw: If + simple present, will + base verb (Type 1: real future condition)
steps.apply: The main clause 'She will feel much better' uses 'will + base verb', confirming a Type 1 conditional. Type 1 conditionals use simple present in the if-clause.
steps.note: This eliminates Options A and C (which use 'will') immediately.

steps.title: Choose between simple present and present continuous
steps.formula_raw: If + simple present, will + base verb (Type 1: real future condition)
steps.apply: Option B 'gets some rest' — simple present, agrees with 'she' (third person singular with -s). Option D 'is getting some rest' — present continuous, describes an action happening now rather than a hypothetical future condition. The conditional context requires simple present.
steps.note: Present continuous in an if-clause would mean 'assuming she is currently resting' which changes the meaning; simple present 'if she gets rest' expresses the intended future condition.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: If + simple present, will + base verb (Type 1: real future condition)
steps.apply: 'She will feel much better if she gets some rest.' — correct Type 1 conditional with simple present in the if-clause and future 'will' in the main clause.

questions.formula_note: A new formula 'conditional-type1' captures the standard Type 1 conditional rule: if + simple present, will + base verb, for real future conditions.

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Modal + Perfect Infinitive
formulas.formula: modal verb + to have + past participle
formulas.meaning: Expresses a present wish, regret, or expectation about an action that did (or did not) happen in the past.
formulas.example: I would like to have visited Paris. She should have completed the form.

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Present Perfect Continuous
formulas.meaning: Describes an action that started in the past and is still continuing or has just finished, emphasising duration or ongoing relevance.
formulas.example: It has been raining since noon. Prices have been rising all week.

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Type 2 Conditional (Hypothetical)
formulas.formula: If + past simple, subject + would/should/could + base verb
formulas.meaning: Describes an unreal or hypothetical situation in the present or future and its imagined result.
formulas.example: If I had more time, I would travel. If he wanted help, he should ask.

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Stative Verb Rule
formulas.formula: stative verbs (want, know, believe, own, love) do NOT use continuous (be + V-ing) tenses
formulas.meaning: Stative verbs describe states, not actions, so they are not used in progressive/continuous forms in standard English.
formulas.example: Correct: I know the answer. Incorrect: I am knowing the answer.

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Tense Consistency (Time Clause Agreement)
formulas.formula: Time clause tense => main clause must use the same or compatible time frame
formulas.meaning: When a subordinate time clause establishes a time reference (past, present, future), the main clause verb must agree with that time frame for grammatical and logical consistency.
formulas.example: As soon as the flight plan was filed, the aircraft was allowed to take off. (both past simple)

formulas.subject: General Aptitude
formulas.name: Type 1 Conditional (Real Future)
formulas.formula: If + simple present, will + base verb
formulas.meaning: Describes a real or likely condition in the future. The if-clause always uses simple present (never 'will') even though the meaning is future.
formulas.example: If she rests, she will feel better. If it rains, we will stay inside.
